title: Needle felted sheep flock
subtitle: A set of three miniature sheep in natural fleece colours for a shelf or windowsill display
excerpt: Needle felt three small sheep in natural Romney or Shetland colours: one white, one moorit brown, one dark grey. Each sheep is about 7 cm long and stands on four wire-core legs. The curly surface texture is built from locks of raw fleece laid over a smooth merino core.
sourceNotes: Miniature sheep sculpture in needle felt is a well-documented genre in UK guild craft instruction and contemporary fibre arts. Method draws on standard body-form technique with wire-core legs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A warm brown fleece colour naturally occurring in Shetland and Soay sheep. Moorit ranges from a pale fawn to a deep chocolate, depending on the individual animal and time of year. The word derives from the Old Norse for heath or moor.

A needle-felting surface technique in which individual unwashed fleece locks are laid onto a felted core and lightly felted at their base only, leaving the tips free to give a curly, natural fleece appearance.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep fingers clear of the needle path at all times.

content.text: Each sheep has a smooth merino core for the body and a
surface built from raw unwashed fleece locks. The
sheep uses naturally coloured brown Shetland fleece. The legs are wire-core to take the weight of the body; the head is a separate ball joined to the body and finished with a black nose.

items.name: White merino roving (body cores and white sheep surface)
Dark grey or Shetland grey roving (grey sheep)
Raw unwashed fleece locks in matching colours (surface texture)
Florist wire, cut into 6 cm lengths (4 legs per sheep)

content.text: For each sheep, roll 12 g of merino roving into a firm oval roughly 6 x 4 cm. Needle-felt all over with the size-36 needle until dense. The core does not need a fine surface as it will be covered with locks.
Step 2: Make and attach wire legs

Wrap four 6 cm wire lengths each in a thin layer of matching roving. Insert one end of each wrapped wire leg into the underside of the body at the appropriate position and needle-felt roving over the insertion point to secure. Stand the sheep upright to check leg placement before the roving is fully secured. Adjust while still easy to move.

Take individual fleece locks and lay them tip-down onto the body surface, overlapping them like roof tiles from the belly upward. Needle-felt each lock at its cut base only, leaving the curly tip free. Work from the belly upward so each row of locks covers the felted base of the row below. The finished body should be completely covered with lock tips.

Roll 3 g of merino into a small oval for the head. Needle-felt to a smooth finish (the face is not covered in locks). Form two small teardrop shapes for ears. Needle-felt the head to the front of the body at a slight forward tilt. Add tiny dark spots for eyes and a small oval of black roving for the nose with the size-38 needle.

items.problem: The sheep tips over rather than standing
items.cause: Legs not spread evenly or wire not stiff enough
items.fix: Use heavier florist wire or double-wrap two wires together for each leg. Check the stance by standing the sheep on a flat surface and adjusting leg angles before the roving sets.

items.problem: The fleece locks fall off the body surface
items.cause: Locks felted only at the very tip rather than at the cut base
items.fix: Always felt at the cut (blunt) base of the lock, not the curly tip. The tip should remain entirely free.

items.problem: The different sheep colours look unnatural or uneven
items.cause: Lock colours mixed rather than kept separate per sheep
items.fix: Keep each colour in a separate pile and use only the correct colour for each sheep. Even a few stray fibres of the wrong colour are visible against the natural tones.
